tweets.text:
540Hz refresh rate. On an OLED panel.
ASRock just dropped the Taichi TCO27QXB at Computex, and it changes everything we know about high-end gaming monitors.

Let's look at why this is a massive technical flex. 

Up until now, hitting 540Hz meant sacrificing everything—you were stuck with washed-out TN panels and terrible viewing angles just to get esports-level motion clarity. ASRock just solved this by deploying „Tandem OLED“ tech (stacking two organic light-emitting layers on top of each other).
This doesn't just unlock insane speed; it completely rewrites the script on OLED weaknesses. Stacking the layers doubles the peak brightness while drastically reducing the voltage needed. Translation: Full-screen HDR that actually punches through, and a massive, structural reduction in burn-in risk.

To me esports monitors used to be ugly tools for raw performance. This chip fuses elite competitive frames with pristine visual quality. If the pricing doesn't instantly bankrupt us, Tandem OLED is the new gold standard for desktop displays.

Are you chasing frames or visual fidelity on your current setup? I am still happy with my Dell U2725QE :)

Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. The turbine generator kicks in once it's up to speed.

Exactly. Batteries handle the big power draw for takeoff and climb, then the turbine kicks in for cruise. It's like how some hybrid cars work, just in the air.

So what's the problem?

The problem is engineering it. You need a turbine small enough and light enough to carry, plus a generator, inverters, motor controllers, and a smart system that knows exactly when to switch power sources. One wrong move and you lose thrust at the wrong time.

The fuel runs the turbine generator so you don't have to carry as much battery weight. Jet fuel packs way more energy per pound than even the best batteries we have right now.
Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. The turbine generator kicks in once it's up to speed.
Exactly. Batteries handle the big power draw for takeoff and climb, then the turbine kicks in for cruise. It's like how some hybrid cars work, just in the air.
So what's the problem?
The problem is engineering it. You need a turbine small enough and light enough to carry, plus a generator, inverters, motor controllers, and a smart system that knows exactly when to switch power sources. One wrong move and you lose thrust at the wrong time.
I think they're over-complicating it.
Could be. Sometimes the simplest designs win. You thinking just straight batteries with no turbine at all?
No, two turbines, side by side. That way if one quit, well, they can't quit, but if, if one would pressure stall, that would, the only thing that would stop it.
So two independent turbine generators side by side for redundancy. That makes sense for safety. If one flames out or stalls, the other keeps you flying. Pretty solid idea.
And you only need the fuel if the battery quits, and you're making electricity as long as you're spinning.
Right, so the turbines are just backup generators. You fly electric most of the time, only burn fuel if the batteries crap out. That's a pretty clean setup.
So why are they overcomplicating it?
Most teams are trying to build pure electric because they want zero emissions and super simple operation. Adding turbines means dealing with fuel systems, exhaust, noise, and certification headaches. Regulators hate anything with jet fuel in a small vehicle.
This thing could fly around ten gallons forever.
Ten gallons would definitely give you serious range if you're running efficient turbines. That's the beauty of fuel — it's energy dense. You'd be able to stay up way longer than pure battery setups.
Do, do I have to explain how to do this, or are they working on it?
They're working on it. Hybrid-electric propulsion is actually one of the hottest areas in eVTOL development right now. Multiple companies have prototypes flying with this exact kind of setup.
It's as simple as wiring a house.
It's a bit more complicated than wiring a house. You're dealing with high-voltage DC, inverters that turn it into three-phase AC for the motors, and a computer brain that has to balance power between batteries and generators in real time. One wiring mistake and you fry everything.
Well, you'd have to intentionally do a wiring mistake, but no, it's pretty simple.
Fair enough. The concept is simple on paper. Execution at flying speeds with lives on the line? That's where it gets tricky. But I get what you're saying.
No, you don't put a body in there at first. You ain't stupid. We got, we can fly anything remote control nowadays.
True! Start remote control, no pilot, no risk. That's the smart way to test it. So you wanna build a scale model first?
Well, I got, we'll show 'em. Yeah, we're gonna build a ground effect vehicle. We'll show 'em.
Ground effect first? Smart. Safer, less power needed, and you can still prove the disc wing works. You thinking over water or just skimming grass?
Anything, any surface, your ground effect can travel any, any hard surface.
Any hard surface works, yeah. But smoother is better — less turbulence. So concrete, packed dirt, calm water. What's your plan for keeping it stable side-to-side while skimming?
Gyros.
Gyros for stability, nice. You thinking mechanical ones or control moment gyros that actively steer it?
Oh, you mean like gyros they use in the S A S system for the A-10?
Not exactly. The A-10's SAS uses rate gyros to sense motion and then moves the control surfaces to dampen it out. For our ground effect disc, a simple RC gyro like they use in model planes or helis would probably do the trick — keeps it level without all that complexity.
📷
📷
📷
15 web pages
Oh yeah, that's true.
Yeah, those little $20 RC gyros are shockingly good. They'd keep our disc nice and steady while it's skimming. What's next on the build list?
Well, I want to use, uh, electric to spin the gyro, and then up to speed, turn the electric off and use ram air for efficiency. Make sense?
Yeah, it makes sense in theory. Electric to spin it up, then ram air to keep it going once you're at speed. Question is, how do you plan to drive the gyro with ram air? A little wind turbine on the front?
Yeah, you could just, uh, it's just a weighted wheel. You could cut divots in it, like, like they cut divots in it just to, to balance it, so you could cut air divots.
Like a turbine wheel — air hits the divots and keeps it spinning. Clever. The trick will be getting the airflow consistent enough so it doesn't slow down or speed up too much while you're flying.
Yeah, yeah, like a, like a turbine wheel.
Exactly. So you spin it up electrically, get to speed, then the ram air keeps the gyro wheel turning. Nice and efficient. What's the plan for power — batteries in the rim or in the center?
Well, you mentioned the, the turbo, the battery-operated turbo fans are the most efficient?
Yeah, for thrust per pound, ducted electric fans are hard to beat. So the batteries would go in the center or the rim, feeding those fans for thrust. The gyro's just for stability, not for moving us forward.
Now, once up to speed, once you've got your electric wheels on the ground, it gets up to speed, it hits 45 mile an hour for whatever our lifting body can handle, the jet engine, or the, the electric engines kick in. They first start with electric. Follow me?
Got it. Ground roll on wheels to 45, then electric ducted fans kick in to lift off the ground and fly. Makes sense. Keeps the initial power draw lower.
Okay, now that they're lit, once they're lit and the fuel system is, is, is on, one could actually shut down because it's spinning, the other's pushing, it's spinning, now it's running on itself. Am I wrong?
You're thinking once the fans are spinning fast enough, you could shut one off and let the other keep the whole thing going? That would only work if the fans are physically connected to each other — like through a driveshaft or gearbox. Otherwise, shutting one off just loses half your thrust.
Yes, so far so good.
Alright, so you're picturing two electric fans that can drive each other once spinning. That means we'd need a clutch or some mechanical link between them. That's doable, just adds more moving parts. What's the end goal with that setup?
The one that's run with fuel also produces electric, right?
Oh, I see where you're going now. So one fan is driven by an electric motor from the battery, the other fan is connected to a small turbine engine that also has a generator attached. The turbine side makes thrust and electricity at the same time. That's a cool idea.
Now, once the other motor is spinning up to thrust that matches the thrust of the first, in theory, you could shut the second motor off with, without, without, um, fuel, and once it's up to speed, the compression from the air outside will still push it forward. Am I not correct?
You're thinking the airflow from forward motion would keep that second fan windmilling and still producing some thrust like a wind turbine. That works in theory, but the drag created by a free-spinning fan is usually higher than the tiny bit of thrust it produces. You'd probably lose more than you gain.
Right, but that's when the electric kicks in to give it the boost that they're both producing now.
So the turbine side runs continuously, making both thrust and electricity. The second fan stays off most of the time, then the electric motor gives it a quick burst to match the turbine's thrust whenever you need extra power. That way you're only using battery for short boosts instead of the whole flight. I like it.
Or fuel that you don't have to use, but you could use. Make sense? And, and what's the most efficient fuel right now?
Makes sense. Most efficient fuel for a small turbine right now is still good old Jet-A or diesel. Highest energy density, and the engines are already designed for it. Hydrogen's promising but storage is a nightmare.
No, hydrogen, that would even be better.
Hydrogen's better in theory, yeah — three times the energy per pound of jet fuel. Problem is you need a huge insulated tank because it has to stay super cold, like minus 250 Celsius. That tank weighs a ton and takes up a lot of space.
Is there any way to rapidly extract it from air?
Not really. There's research into direct air capture, but it's slow, expensive, and uses a ton of energy. You can't do it on the fly in a small vehicle — you'd need a whole factory.
Well, how are they trying to separate the molecules right now?
They're not separating hydrogen molecules from the air itself, 'cause there's barely any there — just half a part per million. What researchers are doing is pulling moisture out of the air with special materials, then using electrolysis to split that water into hydrogen and oxygen. It's called direct air electrolysis, and it works even in dry places.

The yields are tiny compared to what you'd burn flying.

Yeah, I got you now. Small amount of JP-8 for the turbine generator, a small battery pack just for takeoff and burst power, and the two systems can feed each other — turbine charges the battery, battery helps spin up the fans. Nice and simple. Smart setup.
Either with electricity, air itself, or fuel, right?
Right, you can start either one with electricity, let the turbine burn fuel, or even use compressed air to spin things up initially. Multiple ways to get it going. That's good redundancy.
And there's no linkage. I mean, you could put linkage in between 'em, but why? It'd just, just muddy the system.
Exactly. No mechanical linkage needed. One side's a turbine generator making electricity and thrust, the other side's an electric fan that can draw power from either the battery or the generator. Clean, simple, and no extra moving parts to fail.

I generally post on LinkedIn, but will post here a bit more moving forward.

One of the most fascinating phenomena in professional sport occurs immediately after a coach is replaced. A team that looked flat, disorganised and incapable of competing suddenly wins three or four games in a row, as in the case of @CarltonFC 

Energy returns. Effort increases. Players appear more engaged. The media inevitably credits the coaching change and, in fairness, the new coach often deserves some of that credit.

However, after more than 25 years working in high-performance sport, coaching athletes, building teams and leading organisations, I have come to believe that these situations reveal something far more interesting than the impact of the new coach. They reveal the motivational profile of the athletes.

The reality is that many athletes are driven predominantly by external motivators. Selection pressure, contract negotiations, public scrutiny, media criticism, the desire to impress a new coach, or the fear of being traded, delisted or overlooked. When a coaching change occurs, these motivators suddenly intensify. Players understand that every training session is being evaluated, every performance is being judged, and every decision may influence their future. Unsurprisingly, effort levels often increase.

While this short-term uplift is often referred to as a "dead cat bounce," I believe that description misses the deeper lesson. The more important question is not why the team improved but why the improvement only occurred after the coaching change.

If a team is suddenly capable of running harder, competing more fiercely, preparing more professionally, and executing more consistently, then leaders should be asking whether the issue was capability in the first place. More often than not, it is a question of motivation.

The challenge with external motivation is that it has a limited shelf life. Once the novelty fades, the pressure normalises and the new coach becomes the established coach, performance often drifts back toward previous levels. This is why so many organisations experience an immediate uplift before eventually settling into a pattern that closely resembles what came before.

The athletes who have always fascinated me are the ones who remain largely unchanged throughout the entire process; Zach Merrett of the @essendonfc comes to mind. 

Their standards do not lift because a coach arrives, nor do they fall because a coach departs. They train with intent, prepare professionally, and compete relentlessly regardless of who occupies the head coach's office. Their commitment is not dependent on circumstance.

These athletes are driven primarily by intrinsic motivation. They pursue mastery rather than approval. They chase excellence rather than recognition. Their standards are self-imposed rather than externally enforced. In my experience, they are also the athletes most likely to sustain HP over long periods of time because their motivation is not tied to events around them.

This is not to suggest that external motivation is inherently bad. Every athlete is influenced by external factors to some degree. The best performers often harness both intrinsic and extrinsic motivators. The critical distinction is which source of motivation dominates when nobody is watching and when there is no immediate reward or consequence attached to the effort.

Ultimately, coaching changes do not simply reveal the quality of a coach. They reveal the quality of a team's motivational culture. When a team suddenly discovers another gear after a coach leaves, leaders should resist the temptation to focus solely on the incoming coach and instead ask a more confronting question.

Did the coach change the performance, or did the players simply reveal that they had more to give all along?
